target.title: fix(env): propagate service-managed GH_TOKEN to trusted exec
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: `~/.openclaw/.env` could not be used as a durable source for operator-curated tool secrets like `GH_TOKEN` because override-only-dangerous keys were stripped before the managed gateway service could expose them to trusted exec children. - Why it matters: operators can configure `gh` and similar CLI tools for OpenClaw, but after gateway install/restart those tools could not see `GH_TOKEN` through the managed service path, so authenticated CLI workflows failed unless the token was injected some other way. - What changed: allow `~/.openclaw/.env` to retain override-only-blocked secrets as managed service keys, add a trusted exec inherited-env allowlist sourced from `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS`, and document the durable service env behavior. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): everywhere-dangerous env vars such as `LD_PRELOAD`, `NODE_OPTIONS`, `BASH_ENV`, `DYLD_*`, and `GIT_DIR` stay blocked. `OPENCLAW_*` keys still do not gain trusted exec inheritance. Untrusted exec postures do not receive these secrets. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [x] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: `GH_TOKEN` placed in `~/.openclaw/.env` should survive managed gateway install/restart and be available to trusted gateway exec children, so `gh` can authenticate without manually injecting the token into each command. - Real environment tested: macOS OpenClaw gateway installed as a managed service, local OpenClaw checkout from v2026.5.2 with this patch applied, `GH_TOKEN` present in `~/.openclaw/.env`, Telegram/agent exec path using `host=gateway`, `security=full`, `ask=off`. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```sh cp ~/.env ~/.openclaw/.env chmod 600 ~/.openclaw/.env openclaw gateway stop openclaw gateway install --force openclaw gateway start openclaw gateway status ``` then ask the agent to run `gh auth status` and report back - Evidence after fix (screenshot): - Telegram screenshot showing the live exec environment reporting `GH_TOKEN_present: true`, `service_managed_keys_contains_GH_TOKEN: true`, `gh auth status` logged in via `GH_TOKEN`, and `gh api user` returning the authenticated account JSON. Account details and token values are redacted. - Observed result after fix: trusted gateway exec can use `GH_TOKEN` inherited from the managed service env, and `gh` authenticates successfully from the OpenClaw exec environment. - What was not tested: Linux/systemd real-service behavior was not manually tested; the Linux path is covered by the shared service-env/unit tests. Non-trusted exec postures were verified by targeted unit tests, not by a second live service screenshot. The exec integration test (`bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.exec.test.ts`) is skipped on Windows (shell-dependent); unit-level coverage runs on all platforms. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): - `before-1.jpg` and `before-2.jpg` — terminal screenshots showing the pre-fix managed-service behavior where the gateway env path stripped `GH_TOKEN` before it could reach the exec child, so `gh` could not authenticate after service install/restart. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: `~/.openclaw/.env` parsing treated keys that are dangerous as agent request overrides the same as keys that are dangerous everywhere. This stripped operator-curated tool tokens like `GH_TOKEN` before the managed service could record them as durable env. Separately, exec env sanitization had no trusted-mode inherited-key allowlist, so there was no narrow path for managed service keys to reach trusted tool children. - Missing detection / guardrail: existing tests covered dangerous env stripping and service env formatting, but did not cover the desired operator-curated secret path from `~/.openclaw/.env` through managed service metadata into trusted gateway exec. - Contributing context (if known): the security model already distinguished inherited/env override risk, but the durable service env path did not preserve that distinction. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - `src/config/state-dir-dotenv.test.ts` - `src/daemon/service-managed-env.test.ts` - `src/infra/host-env-security.test.ts` - `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.test.ts` - `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.exec.test.ts` - `src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `GH_TOKEN` and similar override-only-blocked keys can be retained from owner-controlled durable service env, become managed service keys, and reach exec children only in the trusted gateway posture (`host=gateway`, `security=full`, `ask=off`). Unsafe keys and `OPENCLAW_*` keys remain blocked. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: unit tests cover each policy seam, and the exec integration test proves the env-composition wiring reaches the child process under the relevant exec postures without requiring live GitHub credentials in CI. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A, new tests were added. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Operators can place `GH_TOKEN` in `~/.openclaw/.env`, reinstall/start the managed gateway, and use `gh` from trusted gateway exec. This does not grant those tokens to untrusted exec postures. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: ~/.openclaw/.env GH_TOKEN -> durable env parser strips override-only-dangerous key -> managed gateway service has no GH_TOKEN-managed allowlist entry -> trusted exec child cannot authenticate gh After: ~/.openclaw/.env GH_TOKEN -> durable env parser keeps operator-curated override-only key -> install records GH_TOKEN in OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS -> trusted gateway exec allowlist permits inherited GH_TOKEN -> gh authenticates from the exec child ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No. - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No. - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: this intentionally allows operator-curated service env secrets like `GH_TOKEN` to reach trusted gateway exec children. The path is constrained to keys recorded by the managed service env metadata and to the trusted exec posture. Everywhere-dangerous keys are still stripped, `OPENCLAW_*` keys are excluded, request overrides remain sanitized, and non-trusted postures do not inherit the allowlisted secret. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: local managed OpenClaw gateway service - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): OpenClaw agent exec path, GitHub CLI (`gh`) as the real CLI consumer - Relevant config (redacted): `GH_TOKEN` present in `~/.openclaw/.env`; `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` included `GH_TOKEN`; token value redacted in all evidence. ### Steps 1. Put `GH_TOKEN` in `~/.openclaw/.env` and set owner-only permissions. 2. Reinstall/restart the managed gateway service with this patch applied. 3. Run a trusted gateway exec env probe and `gh auth status` / `gh api user`. 4. Run the targeted regression tests: ```sh pnpm test src/daemon/service-managed-env.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.exec.test.ts src/config/state-dir-dotenv.test.ts src/infra/host-env-security.test.ts src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.test.ts ``` ### Expected - `process.env.GH_TOKEN` is present inside trusted gateway exec. - `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` contains `GH_TOKEN`. - `gh auth status` reports authentication via `GH_TOKEN`. - Untrusted exec postures do not receive the fixture token in tests. - Everywhere-dangerous and `OPENCLAW_*` keys remain blocked. ### Actual - Live evidence showed `GH_TOKEN_present: true` and `service_managed_keys_contains_GH_TOKEN: true`. - `gh auth status` authenticated via `GH_TOKEN` and `gh api user` succeeded with redacted account output. - Targeted tests covered the parser, managed service allowlist, host env sanitizer, daemon install plan, and exec wiring. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Evidence files prepared for upload: - `before-1.jpg` - `before-2.jpg` - `after-1.jpg` ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Managed gateway install/restart picks up `GH_TOKEN` from `~/.openclaw/.env`. - Trusted gateway exec sees `GH_TOKEN` and `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` includes `GH_TOKEN`. - `gh auth status` and `gh api user` work from the OpenClaw exec environment using `GH_TOKEN`. - Targeted tests exercise trusted, allowlist, and prompted exec postures plus dangerous-key exclusions. - Edge cases checked: - `OPENCLAW_*` keys are excluded from trusted inherited allowlist. - Everywhere-dangerous keys remain blocked even if listed. - PATH override smuggling remains blocked. - Default sanitizer behavior remains strict without `allowInheritedKeys`. - What you did **not** verify: - Manual Linux/systemd live service verification. - Every provider token named in the override-only dangerous list; `GH_TOKEN` is the live proof case and tests use representative fixture keys. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) Yes, for operators who want this behavior. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: place `GH_TOKEN` or similar operator-curated tool secret in `~/.openclaw/.env`, ensure the file is owner-only, then reinstall/restart the managed gateway service so `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` is regenerated. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: secrets that were previously stripped from managed service env can now reach trusted exec children. - Mitigation: only operator-curated durable service env keys are considered, only trusted gateway exec posture receives the inherited allowlist, and dangerous-everywhere plus `OPENCLAW_*` keys remain blocked. - Risk: operators may assume all exec postures receive `GH_TOKEN` after placing it in `~/.openclaw/.env`. - Mitigation: docs state that the exec layer decides inheritance by posture and that these keys are not auto-propagated to untrusted commands.

target.title: feat(tools): introduce group:core for all built-in core tools
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: `group:openclaw` is documented as "all built-in tools" but actually filters out filesystem and shell runtime tools, so there is no obvious `group:*` shorthand for "all built-in core tools." - Why it matters: operators writing `tools.allow: ["group:openclaw"]` expecting broad built-in access get a curated subset instead. A dedicated all-core group name removes the ambiguity. - What changed: added `group:core` as the all-built-in-core-tools shorthand; updated `group:openclaw` docs to accurately say "curated OpenClaw integration tools (excludes filesystem, shell runtime, and provider plugins)"; added `expandToolGroups` to the plugin allowlist normalizer so `group:*` entries work in plugin tool policy. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): no profile semantics changed. `tools.profile: "full"` still means unrestricted, same as unset. Plugin tools are still not included by `group:core`. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: My OpenClaw runs in a non-admin account with it's own credentials. As such when configuring the browser plugin, I discovered that it had to be explicitly added to tools.allow and that I could allow all future configured plugins with `group:plugins` ```bash openclaw config set tools.allow '["group:plugins"]' --strict-json ``` However, when I did this my agent lost access to all core tools because if tools.allow is present it must specify everything allowed. So I went looking for a group similar to group:plugins and found it missing. Now, with this change full yolo looks like this. ```bash openclaw config set agents.defaults.sandbox.mode off openclaw config set tools.profile full openclaw config set tools.exec.ask off openclaw exec-policy preset yolo openclaw config set tools.allow '["group:core","group:plugins"]' --strict-json ``` - Real environment tested: Mac Mini with openclaw v2026.5.2 built from source ```bash git checkout v2026.5.2 git apply seank-group-core.patch pnpm install pnpm ui:build pnpm build ./scripts/package-mac-app.sh pnpm test src/agents/tool-policy.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts pnpm link --global ``` - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```bash openclaw config set tools.allow '["group:core","group:plugins"]' --strict-json openclaw gateway restart ``` - Evidence after fix (screenshot): - Observed result after fix: OpenClaw had access to all core tools as well as browser plugin - What was not tested: I only ran this live on the environment above, beside that just unittest suggested by OpenClaw and GitHub Copilot - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: `group:openclaw` was both the only broad group name and a curated subset, creating a naming gap where no group meant "all built-in core tools." - Missing detection / guardrail: no test asserted the existence of an all-core group or verified that `group:openclaw` excluded fs/runtime tools. - Contributing context (if known): the naming collision was a design clarity problem, not a runtime regression. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - `src/agents/tool-policy.test.ts` - `src/agents/pi-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts` - `src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: - `group:core` expands to built-in core tools. - Plugin optional tools are not enabled by `group:core` alone, but still work with `group:plugins`. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the behavior is pure policy expansion, so unit coverage around the catalog, global tool policy, and plugin allowlist boundary is sufficient. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing group expansion tests covered section groups and `group:openclaw`; this PR adds explicit coverage for `group:core`. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - `tools.allow` / `tools.deny` now support `group:core` for all built-in core tools. - Docs now distinguish: - `tools.profile: "full"`: unrestricted profile, same as unset. - `group:core`: all built-in core tools, excluding plugin tools. - `group:openclaw`: curated OpenClaw integration tools, excluding filesystem, shell runtime, and plugin tools. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: [user wants broad built-in tool access] -> group:openclaw -> curated subset (misleading docs say "all") After: [user wants all built-in core tools] -> group:core -> all built-in core tools [user wants curated integration tools] -> group:openclaw -> curated subset (docs now accurate) [user wants unrestricted profile] -> tools.profile: full -> unrestricted baseline ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: `group:core` is an explicit allow/deny shorthand for existing built-in tools. It does not grant anything unless an operator opts into it in tool policy. Tests lock that it does not enable optional plugin tools by itself. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Windows 11 (rebase + verification); macOS Darwin 25.4.0 arm64 (original patch) - Runtime/container: Node 22, local OpenClaw repo - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): tool policy allowlist examples using `group:core` and `group:plugins` ### Steps 1. Configure `tools.allow: ["group:core"]`. 2. Resolve OpenClaw coding tools. 3. Verify built-in core tools such as `read`, `exec`, `canvas`, `message`, and `cron` are available. 4. Verify optional plugin tools are not enabled by `group:core` alone. ### Expected - `group:core` expands to built-in core tools. - Plugin tools still require explicit plugin/tool allowlist entries or `group:plugins`. ### Actual - Matches expected. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [ ] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Tests after rebase onto current `upstream/main` (59326c8e3b): ```sh pnpm test src/agents/tool-policy.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts ``` ```text ✓ unit-fast src/agents/tool-policy.test.ts (32 tests) 23ms ✓ agents src/agents/pi-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts (55 tests) 779ms ✓ plugins src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts (62 tests) 3773ms Test Files 3 passed (3) Tests 149 passed (149) ``` ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Live gateway with `tools.allow: ["group:core"]` in agent config. - `group:core` is present in the core tool group map (`src/agents/tool-catalog.ts`). - Global allowlist expansion accepts `group:core`. - Optional plugin tools are not unlocked by `group:core` alone. - Docs list `group:core` and no longer describe `group:openclaw` as all built-in tools. - `expandToolGroups` correctly wired into plugin allowlist normalizer (`src/plugins/tools.ts`). - Edge cases checked: - `group:core` plus `group:plugins` still allows optional plugin tools. - Stale group naming references searched across `docs`, `src`, `packages`, and `README.md`. - `group:core` confirmed absent from `upstream/main` before this patch (not previously landed). - What you did **not** verify: - Full CI. - Rendered docs site. ## Review Conversations - [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes - Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): Yes, `group:core` is a new documented tool-policy shorthand. - Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: users might assume `group:core` includes plugin tools. - Mitigation: docs state plugin tools are excluded, and tests verify optional plugins require `group:plugins` or explicit plugin/tool allowlist entries. - Risk: `group:openclaw` documentation could imply broader access than it actually provides. - Mitigation: docs now call it a curated integration group and explicitly distinguish it from `group:core`. ## Attribution - **OpenClaw (Codex 5.5)**: identified the naming gap, proposed the fix, authored the original commit and initial PR description. - **GitHub Copilot (Claude Opus 4.6)**: verified the fix was still viable on current `main`, rebased the commit (resolving 3 merge conflicts including an `expandToolGroups` import relocation to `tool-policy-shared.ts`), ran tests, and rewrote the PR description to match the current template.

target.title: Hooks: chain global pre-commit guard
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw can use the repo-local `git-hooks/pre-commit` through `core.hooksPath=git-hooks`. In that setup, user-level machine-wide Git guards configured via global `core.hooksPath` do not run. That is a local defense gap for checks such as secret scanning and AI-context loss protection.

This PR makes the repo-local pre-commit hook chain the configured global `pre-commit` hook first when it exists and is executable, then continues with the existing OpenClaw staged-file lint/format flow.

Validation evidence:

- `bash -n git-hooks/pre-commit` passed.
- `git-hooks/pre-commit` passed with no staged files.
- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD` passed.
- `git diff --name-status origin/main..HEAD` shows only `git-hooks/pre-commit`.

## Summary

- Problem: this repo can use `core.hooksPath=git-hooks`, which means the machine-wide Git pre-commit guard is bypassed.
- Why it matters: local security and AI-context guards should still run before project-specific formatting hooks.
- What changed: `git-hooks/pre-commit` now invokes the configured global `pre-commit` hook first when present, then continues with the existing staged-file lint/format flow.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no lint/format behavior, dependency, CI, runtime, or product code changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: none

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: the project pre-commit hook now runs the already-configured global pre-commit guard before its existing project checks. The call is conditional on the global hook being present and executable, and it preserves the existing hook behavior afterward.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local shell
- Model/provider: n/a
- Integration/channel (if any): Git hooks
- Relevant config (redacted): global `core.hooksPath` points to a user-level hook directory

### Steps

1. Configure this repository with `core.hooksPath=git-hooks`.
2. Run `bash -n git-hooks/pre-commit`.
3. Run `git-hooks/pre-commit` with no staged files.
4. Compare the PR diff against `origin/main`.

### Expected

- The hook remains syntactically valid.
- With no staged files, the hook exits successfully.
- The PR changes only `git-hooks/pre-commit`.

### Actual

- `bash -n git-hooks/pre-commit` passed.
- `git-hooks/pre-commit` passed.
- `git diff --name-status origin/main..HEAD` shows only `git-hooks/pre-commit`.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Before this PR, a repo-local `core.hooksPath=git-hooks` setup ran the project hook without first invoking the global guard. After this PR, the project hook conditionally invokes the global guard first.

Verification commands run locally:

```bash
bash -n git-hooks/pre-commit
git-hooks/pre-commit
git diff --check origin/main..HEAD
git diff --name-status origin/main..HEAD
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: global pre-commit guard path is called conditionally before the existing project hook logic; no-staged-files path exits successfully.
- Edge cases checked: global hook missing or non-executable remains a no-op because the condition checks both non-empty path and executable file.
- What you did not verify: full `pnpm check` / full test suite, because this only changes a shell pre-commit wrapper.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: n/a

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR or remove the global hook invocation block from `git-hooks/pre-commit`.
- Files/config to restore: `git-hooks/pre-commit`.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: pre-commit exits before running project lint/format if a user-level global hook fails.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a failing user-level global pre-commit hook blocks OpenClaw's project hook.
  - Mitigation: that is intentional for security guard failures; the project hook only chains an executable configured global hook and otherwise preserves existing behavior.

target.title: fix(acp): capture and persist usage_update tokens for ACP sessions
target.body:
## Summary

Plumb token usage from ACP `usage_update` events into the openclaw session record so `totalTokens` is no longer permanently `null` for ACP sessions. Closes catalog #21.

## Bug detail

Every ACP session in the listing reported `totalTokens: null`, `totalTokensFresh: false`, regardless of how many turns it had run. The acpx session record carried `cumulative_token_usage: {}` and `request_token_usage: {}` (empty), confirming token usage was never being persisted from the copilot ACP runtime into the openclaw session entry. Token-cost tracking and budget guards were inoperative for any ACP session.

## How to reproduce

Live repro before fix:

\`\`\`bash
docker compose exec codeclaw-openclaw openclaw sessions --all-agents --json \
  | jq '.sessions[] | select(.key | contains("acp:")) | {key, totalTokens, totalTokensFresh}'
# Before: all ACP sessions show "totalTokens": null, "totalTokensFresh": false
# After: ACP sessions that completed a turn show numeric totalTokens
\`\`\`

Unit-level repro:

\`\`\`bash
git checkout 5e083f0510            # red-test cherry-pick
pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.usage-update-tokens.test.ts
# Before fix: 2 RED (callback never fires; persistence bucket stays unset)
# After fix commit d292fa7199: all 3 GREEN
\`\`\`

## Root cause

`src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.ts:462-474` consumed `usage_update` events for **display only** (`emitSystemStatus(event.text, ...)`). Numeric `event.used` / `event.size` were extracted just for repeat-suppression hashing — never persisted. There was no callback or write path from the projector back to the session record. `resolveSessionTotalTokens` (`src/config/sessions/types.ts:506-514`) reads `entry?.totalTokens` which therefore stayed `null`.

## Fix approach

Catalog Option 1 — write entry-level `totalTokens` via the existing `persistSessionUsageUpdate` helper. No new schema fields:

1. Added optional `onTokenUsage?: (usage: AcpTokenUsage) => void | Promise<void>` parameter to `createAcpReplyProjector`. Existing callers compile unchanged.
2. The projector fires `onTokenUsage({ used, size, total: used })` BEFORE the display dedupe/visibility gate, so persistence always receives the latest values regardless of repeat-suppression. Guarded by `event.tag === "usage_update" && typeof event.used === "number" && typeof event.size === "number"` — text-only `usage_update` events do not trigger the callback.
3. `dispatch-acp.ts` wires the callback to call `persistSessionUsageUpdate({ usage: { input: usage.used, output: 0 }, usageIsContextSnapshot: true, contextTokensUsed: usage.size })`. Because `usageIsContextSnapshot: true`, `deriveSessionTotalTokens` writes the captured value to `entry.totalTokens` — the exact field `resolveSessionTotalTokens` already reads.
4. ACP `usage_update` events do not separate input from output, so all tokens are lumped into `input` (with `output: 0`). The `usageIsContextSnapshot` semantics treat this as a context snapshot rather than incremental usage.

## Tests

- **Red test (now GREEN):** `src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.usage-update-tokens.test.ts` — 3 cases: 2 formerly-red flip GREEN, 1 GREEN control stays GREEN.
- **Adjacent suite:** `src/auto-reply/reply/` — 1538 pass / 0 fail / 115 files. No regressions.
- **Cleanup commit (`1ba8b529a5`):** removed dead pre-fix shim from the test file and added a one-line WHY comment at the projector's `onTokenUsage` call site.

## Catalog reference

Catalog finding: #21. Investigation lives in branch `edpiva/acp-native-session-investigation` (not yet upstream) at `docs/plans/2026-05-08-acp-findings-catalog.md`.

## Verification commands

\`\`\`bash
pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.usage-update-tokens.test.ts   # 3/3 GREEN
pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/                                            # 1538/0
\`\`\`

## Notes for reviewers

- ACP doesn't separate input/output token counts, so the persisted value lumps everything into `input` with `output: 0`. This matches what the catalog finding flagged as the trade-off for option 1; if you'd prefer extending `SessionAcpMeta` with explicit fields instead, I can switch (option 2 in the catalog).
- The optional callback parameter is intentionally additive — no breaking change to existing projector callers.
- `resolveSessionTotalTokens` was reached without modification: option 1 writes `entry.totalTokens` and the existing reader handles it.

---

## Live behavior repro (deployed container)

**Container:** `codeclaw-openclaw:clean` running OpenClaw 2026.5.6 (`e9d4a0a`)

### `totalTokens` permanently `null` for every ACP session

```
$ docker exec codeclaw-openclaw openclaw sessions --all-agents --json \
    | jq -c '.sessions[] | select(.key | startswith("agent:copilot:acp:")) | {key, totalTokens, totalTokensFresh}'

{"key":"agent:copilot:acp:86b7b5af-…","totalTokens":null,"totalTokensFresh":false}
{"key":"agent:copilot:acp:d6fb7506-…","totalTokens":null,"totalTokensFresh":false}
{"key":"agent:copilot:acp:f5ab8dba-…","totalTokens":null,"totalTokensFresh":false}
{"key":"agent:copilot:acp:1f854fa2-…","totalTokens":null,"totalTokensFresh":false}
{"key":"agent:copilot:acp:930316f7-…","totalTokens":null,"totalTokensFresh":false}
{"key":"agent:copilot:acp:63867cf7-…","totalTokens":null,"totalTokensFresh":false}
{"key":"agent:copilot:acp:8fb42bab-…","totalTokens":null,"totalTokensFresh":false}
```

7/7 ACP sessions report `null` even after multiple turns. The acpx record carries `cumulative_token_usage: {}` and `request_token_usage: {}` (empty) — token usage was never plumbed from the copilot ACP runtime into the openclaw entry. Token-cost tracking and budget guards were inoperative for any ACP session.

After this PR (fix commit `d292fa7199`), the projector fires `onTokenUsage` whenever a `usage_update` event lands with numeric `used`/`size`, which triggers `persistSessionUsageUpdate` → `entry.totalTokens` is populated and `resolveSessionTotalTokens` returns the correct value.

target.title: # PR: Filesystem Access Control (PathGuard)
target.body:
# PR: Filesystem Access Control (PathGuard)

## Summary

This PR introduces a stricter, policy-driven filesystem access layer (**PathGuard**) for OpenClaw agent filesystem tools, including local media (image/pdf) loaders.

It closes key security and consistency gaps by:
- canonicalizing requested paths before evaluation (realpath-aware, ENOENT-safe),
- preventing traversal and symlink/junction escapes,
- enforcing `allowedPaths` / `denyPaths` consistently for classic filesystem tools **and** local media loads,
- preserving **deny-overrides-allow** semantics,
- anchoring workspace-relative policy entries to the workspace root (and re-anchoring after realpath),
- treating `allowedPaths: []` as an explicit **deny-all**,
- supporting absolute glob policy entries (via minimatch) alongside canonical absolute literals,
- returning structured, remediation-focused policy denial tool results (`policy_denied`) when access is blocked,
- and enforcing a monotonic filesystem policy ceiling for spawned subagents (global + agent + subagent defaults + spawn-time tightening), plumbed end-to-end into the spawned run.

**Important scope note:**

R: “This PR secures filesystem tools; it does not sandbox arbitrary shell execution.”

Remote URLs (`http(s)://`) and inline `data:` inputs are not treated as filesystem paths and are outside this layer.

---

## Change Type

- [x] Feature
- [x] Security hardening
- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [x] Tests

---

## Linked Issues

- Closes #39672
- Related #8719

---

## Problem Statement (What was wrong before)

Before this PR, filesystem tool enforcement had multiple gaps:

1) **Path canonicalization and symlink escape handling were fragmented**
- Traversal protections and “what path are we actually touching?” logic were spread across tools/flows.
- Non-existent paths (ENOENT) were tricky: naive realpath calls fail and can allow later-creation escapes.

2) **Policy matching was inconsistent**
- Absolute vs relative entries could be evaluated in different “path spaces”.
- Glob vs non-glob entries could diverge in behavior, especially when “roots” filtering was used as an approximation.

3) **Media tools could diverge from file tool policy**
- Local file references inside image/pdf flows could reopen outside-workspace reads if not enforced per resolved file.

4) **Sandbox and host flows lacked a unified enforcement story**
- Enforcement needed to be identical regardless of whether the run is sandboxed or host-based.

5) **Spawned subagents did not have a guaranteed filesystem policy ceiling**
- Subagents needed an explicit, monotonic ceiling that could not be loosened at spawn time.

---

## What Changed (Design + Implementation)

### 1) Core PathGuard enforcement (`src/security/path-guard.ts`)

PathGuard implements:

- **Canonical resolution for existing paths** via `fs.realpath`.
- **Nearest-existing-parent resolution** for ENOENT paths (prevents “create later via symlink” bypass).
- **Symlink/junction-aware comparisons** in canonical path space.
- **Deny precedence**: deny rules always override allow rules.
- **Workspace-only boundary**: `workspaceOnly` blocks outside-workspace access.
- **Relative entry anchoring**: workspace-relative policy entries are anchored to the workspace root, and re-anchored after realpath resolution.
- **Empty allowlists**: `allowedPaths: []` is treated as an explicit deny-all.
- **Absolute policy entries**:
  - absolute **literals** are canonicalized before comparison,
  - absolute **globs** are supported (matched against canonical real paths) without attempting to realpath the pattern.
- **Glob detection** via `Minimatch(...).hasMagic()` (brace/extglob supported).

### 2) Filesystem tool integration (read/write/edit/apply_patch-style)

- Tool FS config is resolved through the tool/fs policy configuration and enforced using `checkPathGuardStrict(...)`.
- Wrappers normalize args and run PathGuard checks on resolved paths.
- `apply_patch` is explicitly guarded by parsing patch hunks and enforcing FS policy on each patch target path (not just args.path/file_path).

### 3) Structured `policy_denied` tool result (model guidance)

When PathGuard denies access, tools return a structured tool result (instead of only throwing a raw error) that:
- states the restriction is system-enforced and cannot be bypassed,
- describes what the tool attempted to do,
- provides actionable remediation options,
- and explicitly points to config keys that can be changed to grant access.

This improves model behavior: fewer useless retries, more direct user remediation.

### 4) Media tool integration (image/pdf local paths)

Local media loads are now policy-checked on the resolved file path:
- `src/agents/tools/image-tool.ts`
- `src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.ts`

Key invariant:
- enforcement is per resolved path using `checkPathGuardStrict(...)`, not via root-prefix approximations.

### 5) Sandbox behavior

For local media loads in sandbox mode:
- PathGuard runs in sandbox flows too,
- policy root uses `sandbox.root` when sandboxed,
- otherwise `workspaceDir` is used.

### 6) Subagent filesystem policy ceiling (spawn-time tightening + runtime enforcement)

Goal: spawned subagents must never receive broader filesystem access than the configured ceiling.

Policy sources (monotonic tightening):
- Global: `tools.fs`
- Per-agent: `agents.<id>.tools.fs`
- Subagent defaults/ceiling: `tools.subagents.fs`
- Sessions-spawn defaults/ceiling: `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (applied when the tool call omits `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`)
- Spawn-time tightening: `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`

Merge semantics:
- `workspaceOnly`: OR
- `denyPaths`: UNION
- `allowedPaths`: enforced as a ceiling/tightening model (empty list = deny all)

Persistence + plumbing:
- Effective policy is persisted on the spawned child session (`spawnedToolFsPolicy`) via `sessions.patch`.
- Gateway plumbs it into the child run as ingress field `toolFsPolicy`.
- Gateway session resets (`/new` / `/reset`) preserve `spawnedToolFsPolicy` (no widening after reset).
- Embedded runner passes it into tool construction.
- `createOpenClawCodingTools` accepts an `fsPolicy` override and uses it as the effective policy for that run.
- Nested spawns inherit the parent session’s `spawnedToolFsPolicy` ceiling before applying config/tool-call tightening.

---

## Configuration (for contributors)

### Filesystem policy knobs

- `tools.fs.workspaceOnly: boolean`
- `tools.fs.allowedPaths: string[] | undefined` (empty array means deny-all)
- `tools.fs.denyPaths: string[] | undefined`

Subagent ceiling:
- `tools.subagents.fs.{workspaceOnly, allowedPaths, denyPaths}`

Sessions-spawn defaults + spawn-time tightening:
- `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (config default ceiling/tightening)
- `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (tool arg tightening; can only further restrict)

Notes:
- Deny always wins.
- Relative entries are workspace-anchored.
- Policies are evaluated against canonicalized real paths.

---

## Explicit Non-Goals / Out of Scope

- Exec sandboxing is not implemented here.
  R: “This PR secures filesystem tools; it does not sandbox arbitrary shell execution.”
- No shell argument parsing.
- Remote source governance is out-of-scope.

---

## Implementation Notes (for contributors)

### Policy evaluation edge cases covered

- `allowedPaths: []` is treated as an explicit **deny-all** (not “no restriction”).
- Relative literal entries are **re-anchored after realpath**: if a workspace-relative entry canonicalizes outside the workspace (e.g. it points at a symlink to an external dir), it **does not** match outside-workspace targets.
- Absolute glob entries support symlink-alias authoring by **canonicalizing the non-glob prefix** before matching.
  - When rebuilding the rewritten pattern, the implementation **normalizes slash joining** so root-anchored globs stay semantically equivalent (e.g. `"/**/*.pem"` must not become `"//**/*.pem"`).

### Tool behavior on denial (model guidance)

When a filesystem access is blocked by PathGuard, tools return a structured `policy_denied` result (English text + structured fields) so models:
- do not attempt “bypass retries”,
- clearly communicate what was attempted,
- present actionable remediation options,
- and point to config keys (`tools.fs.*`) for operators to change if access is intended.

### Subagent filesystem ceiling (monotonic tightening)

Effective subagent policy is computed as a ceiling and is monotonic (cannot be widened by spawn calls):
- global: `tools.fs`
- agent: `agents.<id>.tools.fs`
- subagent defaults/ceiling: `tools.subagents.fs`
- sessions-spawn defaults: `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`
- spawn-time tightening: `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` / tool arg `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`

Merge intent:
- `workspaceOnly`: OR
- `denyPaths`: UNION
- `allowedPaths`: ceiling/tightening model (empty list = deny all)

The effective ceiling is persisted on the child session (`spawnedToolFsPolicy`) and forwarded into the embedded runner (`toolFsPolicy` → `createOpenClawCodingTools({ fsPolicy })`).

---

## Verification

- CI checks and tests pass (per latest runs).
- Added/maintained coverage ensuring `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` is applied as a default tightening when the tool call omits `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`.
- Verified critical denial scenarios: traversal, symlink/junction escapes, ENOENT parent-walk, deny precedence, empty allowlist deny-all, sandbox media enforcement, apply_patch enforcement, and subagent ceiling propagation.
- Verified monotonic nested spawning: a tightened subagent cannot spawn a child with broader FS access (parent `spawnedToolFsPolicy` is inherited).
- Verified gateway resets (`/new` / `/reset`) preserve the spawned FS ceiling (no widening after reset).

---

## Reviewer / Contributor Notes (Do not regress these invariants)

- Do not replace per-path enforcement with root-prefix approximations for glob policies.
- Keep deny-overrides-allow.
- Keep relative policy entries anchored to workspace (including after realpath).
- Keep sandbox and host local-file enforcement aligned.
- Keep local filesystem policy and remote-source policy separate concepts.
- Keep subagent policy tightening monotonic (no privilege escalation vs ceiling).
- Remember: exec is outside this enforcement layer.

---

## Rollback / Recovery

- Disable restrictions by setting `workspaceOnly: false` and clearing `allowedPaths` / `denyPaths`.
- No data migration required.

target.title: [codex] Cron: repair empty sanitized final replies
target.body:
﻿???## Summary

- Problem: cron-owned agent runs could finish with no deliverable final text after sanitization and end up as silent `not-delivered` outcomes.
- Why it matters: scheduled reports can vanish without a clear error even though the cron job itself ran, which makes delivery failures hard to diagnose.
- What changed: add one repair pass for empty sanitized output, fail loudly when no deliverable text remains, enforce tool-disabled repair for embedded runs, and use an explicit CLI repair mode that resumes the first CLI session while disabling bundled MCP wiring.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not relax reasoning-tag sanitization, does not change normal non-cron agent turns, and does not address the legacy local WhatsApp health-check cron issue tracked separately in #60204.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Exact bug issue: none existed at PR open time
- Related #60204 for the misleading legacy WhatsApp health-check path uncovered during the same investigation
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: cron delivery could receive an empty final text after sanitization (for example when malformed reasoning tags consumed the visible reply), but the cron runner had no recovery pass and the delivery path did not convert that condition into a hard error.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the runner did not retry with a constrained no-tools finalization prompt, and the direct-delivery path treated missing synthesized text too softly.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the existing sanitization behavior is intentional; the gap was in cron finalization and delivery handling, not in the sanitizer itself.
- Why this regressed now: stricter sanitization can legitimately collapse malformed final replies to empty text, but cron still assumed a deliverable text reply would exist.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: ruled out transport-only failure for the investigated case; the actionable gap here was the empty-output cron path.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts`, `src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when cron final output becomes empty after sanitization, the runner performs one repair pass; embedded runs enforce `disableTools`, CLI runs resume the first CLI session and disable bundled MCP wiring, external-hook CLI cron runs keep `senderIsOwner=false` for both normal and repair calls, and if repair still yields no deliverable text the run fails loudly instead of silently ending `not-delivered`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in cron run finalization plus delivery dispatch, and the narrowest stable guardrail is to exercise those exact seams with mocked agent output.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing interim-retry coverage already covered acknowledgement retries but not empty sanitized output.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Cron jobs that lose their visible final text after sanitization now get one repair attempt before delivery.
- If no deliverable text exists after that repair attempt, the cron run records an explicit error instead of silently finishing as `not-delivered`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [delivery path sees no text] -> [silent not-delivered]

After:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [no-tools repair pass]
          -> [recovered text -> deliver] or [still empty -> explicit cron error]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: the embedded repair path enforces `disableTools`; the CLI repair path is explicitly not represented as native-tool-free, resumes the first CLI session, and disables OpenClaw-bundled MCP wiring. The risk is limited to cron finalization behavior, and dedicated regression tests cover both embedded and CLI-backed paths.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node + pnpm checkout
- Model/provider: mocked embedded + CLI runner paths in targeted tests
- Integration/channel (if any): cron + Telegram announce path in regression coverage
- Relevant config (redacted): default test fixtures

### Steps

1. Run `pnpm test -- src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts`
2. Run `pnpm test -- src/cron/isolated-agent.skips-delivery-without-whatsapp-recipient-besteffortdeliver-true.test.ts`
3. Run `pnpm test -- src/agents/cli-runner.prepare.test.ts`

### Expected

- Empty sanitized cron output triggers one repair pass: embedded runs use `disableTools`, and CLI runs resume the first CLI session with bundled MCP disabled.
- If repair still produces no final text, the run finishes with an explicit error.
- CLI-backed repair mode does not inject bundled MCP overlays.

### Actual

- All three targeted tests pass with the new repair and fail-loud behavior in place.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: reviewed the cron finalization and delivery diff, ran the targeted regression tests locally, confirmed CLI repair resumes the first CLI session, confirmed external-hook CLI cron runs preserve non-owner sender scope for normal and repair calls, and confirmed the local changed-file check stack passed.
- Edge cases checked: empty payloads without descendants, repair success, repair failure, CLI repair-mode bundled MCP disablement, and first CLI session reuse during repair.
- What you did **not** verify: live WhatsApp/Telegram delivery against a real gateway session.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Cron-owned CLI agent runs that produce no deliverable final text now perform one repair pass and record the repaired final text instead of silently ending without a user-visible result. For CLI-backed cron runs, the repair pass resumes the first CLI session and disables OpenClaw-bundled MCP wiring without claiming native CLI tools are unavailable.

Real environment tested: Windows/PowerShell checkout. The live repair proof below was run at repair head `0f1b25fbfc`; current pushed head `d6ed463c96` keeps that repair path, additionally restores external-hook CLI non-owner sender scope, includes the upstream lint repair needed for the final CI head, and addresses the Greptile duplicate-descendant-predicate review note; verified with targeted red/green tests plus `pnpm lint --threads=8`. Local OpenClaw Gateway was at `ws://127.0.0.1:19162` with isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`; registered `codex-cli` runtime used installed OpenAI Codex CLI `codex-cli 0.130.0-alpha.5` through a thin proof wrapper around `codex exec --output-last-message`; cron delivery was disabled intentionally with `--no-deliver`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
corepack pnpm openclaw gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron add --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --name "PR60212 fixed repair marker proof" --at "2030-01-08T00:00:00Z" --keep-after-run --session isolated --message "Real behavior proof for PR 60212. If the prompt you are reading contains the phrase Repair it now, reply exactly OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK. Otherwise return an empty final answer and do not output any visible text." --no-deliver --timeout-seconds 300 --model openai/gpt-5.5 --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron run 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --expect-final --timeout 360000

corepack pnpm openclaw cron show 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron runs --id 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --limit 5 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted>
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ corepack pnpm openclaw gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json
{
  "ok": true,
  "eventLoop": { "degraded": false, "reasons": [] },
  "plugins": { "loaded": ["acpx", "browser", "canvas", "device-pair", "file-transfer", "memory-core", "openai", "phone-control", "talk-voice"], "errors": [] }
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron run 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --expect-final --timeout 360000
{
  "ok": true,
  "enqueued": true,
  "runId": "manual:3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091:1778695539774:2"
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron show 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json
{
  "id": "3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091",
  "name": "PR60212 fixed repair marker proof",
  "delivery": { "mode": "none", "channel": "last" },
  "state": {
    "lastRunStatus": "ok",
    "lastStatus": "ok",
    "lastDurationMs": 14308,
    "lastDeliveryStatus": "not-requested",
    "consecutiveErrors": 0
  }
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron runs --id 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --limit 5 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted>
{
  "entries": [
    {
      "jobId": "3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091",
      "action": "finished",
      "status": "ok",
      "summary": "OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK",
      "runId": "manual:3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091:1778695539774:2",
      "durationMs": 14308,
      "model": "gpt-5.5",
      "provider": "codex-cli",

```

Observed result after fix: The real local gateway accepted the cron job, ran it through the registered `codex-cli` backend, recovered `OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK` during the repair flow, and recorded the run as `status: "ok"`. Delivery was not requested by design because the proof command used `--no-deliver`; the current code keeps the CLI mode explicit, resumes the first CLI session for repair, and does not hit the earlier native-tool fail-closed error.

What was not tested: External WhatsApp/Telegram send was not exercised; delivery was disabled to keep the local proof isolated and non-invasive.
Current-head ownership, lint, and Greptile cleanup verification after commit `d6ed463c96`:

target.title: fix(gateway): preserve indentation when recombining non-streaming replies
target.body:
AI-assisted: yes (OpenClaw agent-authored).
Testing: targeted local validation (gateway regression tests + targeted lint + pre-commit repo checks).

## Summary

- Companion to merged #55960, not a duplicate: #55960 fixed indentation loss while stripping inline reply directives in `src/utils/directive-tags.ts`; this PR fixes the remaining gateway-layer loss when non-streaming reply parts are recombined in `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`.
- Preserve each non-streaming payload chunk as authored when recombining it, so indentation-sensitive content survives payload boundaries.
- Reuse that same combiner for `/btw` side results so sibling non-streaming gateway paths behave consistently.
- Normalize `CRLF` / `CR` / `LF` boundary trimming and add focused regression coverage for the single-newline, `/btw`, and CRLF cases.
- Scope boundary: no streaming-path formatting changes, no Markdown parsing changes, and no channel-specific formatting rules.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #42576
- Related #29377
- Companion to merged #55960

## Relationship to #55960

Merged PR #55960 fixed one real whitespace-loss layer: inline reply-directive stripping in the shared parser (`src/utils/directive-tags.ts`).

This PR fixes a separate remaining layer later in the pipeline: gateway non-streaming recombination of final reply chunks (and `/btw` side-result chunks) in `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`.

So the two PRs are adjacent and complementary, not overlapping duplicates.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Non-streaming final replies and `/btw` side results now preserve indentation when formatted content spans multiple payloads. When one chunk already ends with a single newline and the next chunk does not start with one, the gateway now preserves that authored single newline instead of forcing a blank line. The combiner also trims `CRLF` / `CR` / `LF` cleanly at outer boundaries so Windows-style line endings do not leak stray carriage returns.

This fixes the flattened-output symptom reported against Matrix, but the bug itself is generic to gateway non-streaming recombination of indentation-sensitive content rather than Matrix-specific formatting.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node.js checkout
- Model/provider: n/a (gateway-side reply recombination)
- Integration/channel (if any): reproduced against the gateway non-streaming `chat.send` path; user report was on Matrix
- Relevant config (redacted): n/a

### Steps

1. Produce a non-streaming assistant reply whose formatted block is emitted across multiple final payloads.
2. Include indentation-sensitive content such as nested YAML, a nested list, or a fenced code block.
3. Observe the stored/broadcast final message text.

### Expected

- Indentation from each payload chunk is preserved in the final combined reply.

### Actual

- Leading whitespace at payload boundaries was trimmed away, flattening structured content.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Added focused regression coverage for:
- the authored single-newline separator case
- an indentation-sensitive `/btw` YAML side result
- `CRLF` boundary trimming

The linked user issue includes before/after screenshots of the real-world symptom.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: targeted gateway regression test for the single-newline recombination edge case; targeted `/btw` side-result regression test for an indented YAML block; targeted `CRLF` boundary regression coverage; targeted lint on the changed files; repo pre-commit checks during commit.
- Edge cases checked: whitespace-only payload chunks are still ignored; separator insertion still only happens when adjacent chunks are not already separated by line breaks; authored single-newline separators are preserved instead of expanded to blank lines; `CRLF` boundaries do not leave stray carriage returns.
- What you did **not** verify: live Matrix delivery against a running instance, broader streaming reply behavior outside the existing `/btw` block-side-result coverage, or full-repo test suites.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert commits `6b2461a11`, `c753cd0af`, and `d667f18f1` (or revert the PR branch changes as a unit)
- Files/config to restore: `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts`, and `src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: unexpected extra blank lines between merged payload chunks, unexpected loss of an authored single newline between chunks, stray carriage returns from `CRLF` boundaries, or lost indentation in `/btw` side results

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: preserving authored separators changes the old trim/join behavior for payloads that already end in a single newline.
  - Mitigation: this is intentional, explicitly regression-tested, and still bounded by the same whitespace-only-chunk skipping plus separator insertion only when neither side already provides a line break.
- Risk: preserving chunk text could expose `CRLF` edge cases at outer boundaries.
  - Mitigation: the combiner now trims `CRLF` / `CR` / `LF` consistently at the start/end, with targeted regression coverage.
- Risk: reviewers may mistake this PR for a duplicate of #55960.
  - Mitigation: this description now names the exact layer split: `#55960` fixes directive parsing, this PR fixes gateway non-streaming recombination.

target.title: fix(agents): warm context-window cache for the gateway daemon
target.body:
## What

The long-running gateway daemon never warms the context-window cache, so synchronous lookups on the status path return `DEFAULT_CONTEXT_TOKENS = 200_000` until the first agent run completes. That value then gets persisted onto the session record and ratchets every subsequent model switch to the wrong context window.

## How to reproduce

1. Configure `agents.defaults.models["github-copilot/claude-opus-4.6-1m"] = { alias: "opus1m" }` (Copilot's `/models` advertises 1,000,000 input tokens for this id).
2. Restart the gateway daemon.
3. In a fresh session, run `📊 session_status` immediately. It reports `Context: …/200k` instead of `…/1000k`.
4. Switch to `claude-opus-4.6` (`/model opus`) - it also reports 200K, even though pi-ai's catalog has it at 1M.
5. Switch back to `claude-opus-4.6-1m` - still 200K.

The 200K comes from `lookupContextTokens(...) ?? DEFAULT_CONTEXT_TOKENS` in `getSessionDefaults` and the equivalent in `status-message.ts`. The persisted value is then read by subsequent status calls via `entry.contextTokens`, so the wrong window sticks until something explicitly clears or overwrites it.

## Root cause

The gateway daemon launches via systemd as:

```
ExecStart=/usr/bin/node /home/.../dist/index.js gateway --port 18789
```

Which means `argv[0]="node"`, `argv[1]="/home/.../dist/index.js"`, `argv[2]="gateway"`.

Two things conspire to disable eager cache warmup for the daemon:

1. `isLikelyOpenClawCliProcess` only matches `argv[1]` basenames `openclaw`, `openclaw.mjs`, `entry.js`, `entry.mjs`. The bundled `dist/index.js` entry is not recognised, so the function returns `false`.
2. `SKIP_EAGER_WARMUP_PRIMARY_COMMANDS` includes `"gateway"`. Even when the recognition above is fixed, the primary command `gateway` would still be skipped.

So `shouldEagerWarmContextWindowCache()` returns `false` for the daemon and the cache load is never kicked off at startup. Every `allowAsyncLoad: false` lookup returns `undefined` until the first agent run happens to warm the cache through some other path - by which point the 200K fallback has already been persisted onto the session record.

## Fix

- Recognise `index.js` / `index.mjs` as legitimate OpenClaw entry basenames in `isLikelyOpenClawCliProcess`.
- Drop `gateway` from `SKIP_EAGER_WARMUP_PRIMARY_COMMANDS`. The warmup is async and best-effort, so short-lived `openclaw gateway status` / `gateway restart` CLI invocations don't pay any blocking cost.

I left a comment block in the skip list explaining why `gateway` must not return - this is the kind of thing that's easy to add back without realising the daemon depends on it.

## Test plan

- New unit tests in `src/agents/context.test.ts` cover:
  - the gateway daemon argv shape (`node dist/index.js gateway --port ...`)
  - the CLI `gateway status` shape
  - help/version invocations (still skipped)
  - generic plugin-sdk-style imports from random Node scripts (still skipped, since `index.js` is too generic to match without an OpenClaw-specific path)
- Manually verified locally: after rebuilding and restarting the gateway, `📊 session_status` reports the correct context window for each Copilot Claude variant on a fresh session, and switching between models (`/model opus`, `/model opus1m`, `/model opus47`) updates the displayed window correctly instead of pinning at 200K.

## Notes

There's a separate, narrower issue in `applyDiscoveredContextWindows` where the cache stores bare model ids and uses min-of-all-providers. That can still under-report when the same bare id appears under both `anthropic` (200K) and `github-copilot` (1M) - but with the daemon warmup fixed, the qualified-then-bare lookup chain in `resolveContextTokensForModel` finds the right value first for the cases I've seen. Happy to follow up with a per-provider key if maintainers want.

target.title: [codex] fix: allow trusted .openclaw symlink

## Summary

Closes #72572.

This restores support for dotfile-managed `~/.openclaw` directories while keeping exec approval paths hardened on both the Node and macOS node-host paths.

- allow trusted first-level `~/.openclaw` symlinks on POSIX after validating link ownership, link location, target ownership, target permissions, and stable resolved ancestors
- keep Windows `.openclaw` symlink targets blocked until ACL validation exists
- keep rejecting user-controlled chained symlink targets, deeper symlink traversal, and symlinked `exec-approvals.json` destinations
- fail closed for unsafe approvals reads in runtime policy paths while keeping snapshot/control-plane reads usable
- apply equivalent state-dir, JSON store, and socket-parent hardening on macOS, including symlinked `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` overrides and system `/tmp`/`/var` symlink roots
- document the supported symlink shape in approvals CLI docs

target.title: feat: server-rendered status footer and context threshold warnings
target.body:
## Summary

Server-rendered status footer and one-shot context-usage warnings for outbound chat messages. Fixes the "model lies about its own runtime state" failure mode in #71984 by moving footer authorship below the LLM.

## Why

Asking a model to append a status footer like `📚 X% (Xk/200k) · 🧹 N compactions · 🧠 model` to its own messages is structurally unreliable. Logged production regressions show the model writing `5% (10k/200k)` while actual context was `134% (268k/200k)`. Three escalating prompt rules failed to prevent this. Any field the model writes about its own runtime is fabricable; the runtime knows the truth and should write it.

## What ships

### Feature A: footer renderer + stripper

- New pure module `src/utils/outbound-footer.ts` exporting `stripFabricatedFooter`, `renderFooter`, `evaluateContextWarning`, and the composite `processOutboundText` hook.
- `messages.outboundFooter.enabled` (bool) and `messages.outboundFooter.template` (string) on `MessagesConfig`.
- The outbound pipeline in `sendMessage` always strips any model-written footer matching the canonical pattern, regardless of config. There is no opt-out for the strip step. When the renderer is enabled, a server-authored footer is appended in its place using live values from session state.
- Supported placeholders: `{context_pct}`, `{context_tokens}`, `{context_limit}`, `{compactions}`, `{model_alias}`. Unknown placeholders are left as the literal `{name}` form so config typos stay visible.

### Feature B: context threshold warning

- `messages.contextWarning.enabled` (bool) and `messages.contextWarning.thresholds` (number array, default `[70, 85, 95]`).
- When usage crosses the highest unwarned threshold for the session, the hook prepends a single line:
  ```
  ⚠️ Context 90% - consider /new
  ```
- Warning state is tracked on `SessionEntry.contextWarningThresholdsTriggered`, so each threshold fires exactly once per session.

### Pipeline placement

The hook lives in `src/infra/outbound/footer-hook.ts` and is wired into `sendMessage` before the outbound payload plan is built. It mirrors the lazy-loading pattern already used by the message gateway runtime so importing it does not pull a heavy session-store dependency tree into every consumer. Failures fall through to the original text: the hook never blocks delivery.

## Tests

- `src/utils/outbound-footer.test.ts` covers the stripper, the renderer, and the threshold-warning evaluator (22 tests).
- Full `unit-fast` project: 6558 tests passing.
- Full `infra` project: 2543 tests passing.
- Full `auto-reply` project: 1598 tests passing.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:extensions` clean.
- `pnpm build` clean.

## Docs

New page at `docs/concepts/outbound-footer.md` covering setup, placeholders, and where the values come from.

## Acceptance criteria from #71984

- [x] Config schema for `footer.template` with documented variables
- [x] Runtime renders/appends footer on outbound channel messages when configured
- [x] Variables sourced from same telemetry as `session_status` (context tokens, compactions, model alias)
- [x] Per-channel enable/disable: yes via the standard `channels.<id>` overrides on `messages.outboundFooter`
- [ ] Optional `[[footer]]` directive variant for explicit placement: not implemented in this PR. The hook always appends at end-of-message when enabled. Happy to add the directive variant in a follow-up if reviewers want it.
- [x] Docs page covering setup + variable reference

Closes #71984.
